figures.gloss: The invisible spirit population of Hausaland — hundreds of named personalities dwelling in tamarind trees, anthills, borrow-pits, and the bush, organized into houses and lineages that mirror human society. They afflict the unwary with illness and madness, and reward their tamed mounts with healing power and standing.
figures.facet: A pantheon that never closed its rolls: Fulani herders, Muslim clerics, and in the 1920s even European colonial officers (the Babule spirits) joined the iskoki — the spirit world as a running census of everyone who ever held power over the Hausa.
figures.provenance: A. J. N. Tremearne, The Ban of the Bori (1914); Michael Onwuejeogwu, 'The Cult of the Bori Spirits among the Hausa' (1969); Fremont Besmer, Horses, Music, and Gods (1983); Adeline Masquelier, Prayer Has Spoiled Everything (2001) on the Babule episode.

figures.gloss: The great division of the pantheon: the farfaru, 'white' Muslim spirits — clerics, emirs, pious Fulani — and the babbaku, 'black' pagan spirits of the bush and the old Maguzawa order. A full bori house keeps both moieties and dances both.
figures.facet: Islamization recorded from the inside: rather than deny the new faith or be erased by it, the pantheon split into a Muslim moiety and a pagan one — conversion mapped directly onto the spirit world.
figures.provenance: Besmer's Kano fieldwork (Horses, Music, and Gods, 1983); Tremearne (1914).

figures.gloss: The curing-initiation that turns affliction into vocation: the sick person is diagnosed as ridden by particular iskoki, secluded, and taught each spirit's rhythms, gestures, and dance, emerging as a 'mare of the gods' — permanently married to the spirits that once tormented her.
figures.facet: Bori's therapeutic bargain in miniature — the spirit is never cast out but domesticated into a lifelong, fee-earning partnership; illness becomes office.
figures.provenance: Besmer (1983) documents the Kano girka in full; Tremearne (1914); Mary Smith, Baba of Karo (1954).

figures.gloss: The title of the woman at the head of a bori community — organizer of the dances, diagnostician of spirit-caused illness, and protector of the unmarried, divorced, and otherwise 'independent' women who fill the cult's ranks. In Gobir, bori historically stood under the protection of the royal Inna, the 'mother' of the state.
figures.facet: Institutionalized female religious authority persisting inside an emirate order that grants women no public religious office — the cult as a parallel government of the marginalized.
figures.provenance: Tremearne (1914); Mary Smith, Baba of Karo (1954); Masquelier (2001) on Niger; Nicole Echard's Ader fieldwork.

figures.gloss: One of the best-loved iskoki: the handsome, open-handed young noble who gambles away fortunes and keeps mistresses — his possessed mounts strut, deal imaginary cards, and scatter money to the crowd.
figures.facet: The pantheon as social portraiture: bori keeps a spirit for every recognizable Hausa type, and the wastrel prince is held up to the community in a possessed body — admired, indulged, and quietly judged at once.
figures.provenance: Besmer (1983) records his praise-songs and possession behaviour in Kano; Tremearne (1914) lists him among the principal spirits.

edges.note: The rite exists for the winds: girka turns an iska's affliction into a governed, lifelong alliance — no initiation without a tormenting spirit to tame.

GOLD SEAM: under Islam the iskoki survive by reclassification as aljanu — the Qur'anic jinn-class to which Iblis belongs — orthodoxy's own demonology becoming the cover under which the old pantheon persists.

GOLD SEAM: bori's map of its own Islamization — the order of Allah absorbed as the 'white' Muslim moiety of the pantheon while the pagan 'black' spirits keep the bush; a tension seam, not an equation.

Neighbouring West African possession idioms — Ṣàngó mounts his elegun as the iskoki ride their girka-made 'mares'; Hausa and Yoruba are unrelated language families, so the kinship is regional-typological, never cognate.

The zar-bori complex (I. M. Lewis): twin African cults of affliction that heal by initiation and accommodation rather than exorcism; diffusion between them is argued — the Sudanese branch is even called 'zar bori' — but unproven, so the seam stays typological.
Women ritual masters of twin possession cults — the Magajiya and the shaykha are both graduated patients who became physicians of the spirit world.
Two beloved wastrel spirits — the gambling Hausa prince and the drinking Burmese playboy nat — possession pantheons on opposite sides of the world each keeping a spirit for the charming prodigal.

Both cults cure by adoption, not expulsion — the girka tames the iska into a lifelong mount-and-rider marriage exactly as the zar bargain converts affliction into contract; typological twins in Lewis's zar-bori complex, historical diffusion unproven.

dossier.essence: Bori is the spirit-possession cult of the Hausa-speaking peoples of northern Nigeria and southern Niger — the surviving ritual face of the pre-Islamic iskoki ('winds') religion, carried on inside an overwhelmingly Muslim society. Its adepts, most of them women, are ritually bound to the spirits that first afflicted them and serve as their 'mounts' in public possession dances that diagnose, heal, and entertain.
dossier.cosmos: The world is shared with hundreds of named iskoki — invisible spirits dwelling in trees, anthills, and the bush, organized into houses and lineages that mirror Hausa society and divided into the 'white' Muslim farfaru and the 'black' pagan babbaku. Under Islam they persist reclassified as aljanu, the Qur'anic jinn, and illness, madness, and misfortune are their work; the remedy is alliance, never exorcism. Bori proposes no afterlife of its own: Muslim adepts hold Islam's eschatology, while the cult governs relations with the spirit world in this life.
dossier.practice: The core institution is the girka, a curing-initiation in which the afflicted person is secluded and taught the rhythms, gestures, and character of each spirit that rides her, emerging as a lifelong adept. In public performance, musicians on the garaya lute or goge fiddle call each spirit by its praise-song until the adepts fall possessed and enact its persona for fees; titled figures — the Magajiya at the head of the bori women, the Sarkin Bori — organize communities drawn heavily from women and the socially marginal.
dossier.canon: No scripture — an oral corpus of spirit praise-epithets (kirari), songs, and drum-rhythms; the ethnographic record runs from Tremearne (1913-14) through Besmer's Kano study and Masquelier's Niger fieldwork.
dossier.reflects: The conversion of affliction into office — possession institutionalized as a licensed career through which the marginalized gain healing authority, income, and a public voice.
sources: A. J. N. Tremearne, 'The Ban of the Bori: Demons and Demon-Dancing in West and North Africa' (1914)
Fremont E. Besmer, 'Horses, Musicians and Gods: The Hausa Cult of Possession-Trance' (1983)
Adeline Masquelier, 'Prayer Has Spoiled Everything: Possession, Power, and Identity in an Islamic Town of Niger' (2001)
Mary F. Smith, 'Baba of Karo: A Woman of the Muslim Hausa' (1954)
